Clinical Research Whitepaper

Health Intelligence Infrastructure for New Orleans, Louisiana

New Orleans is BLEU's home. The most complex health ecosystem in America. This whitepaper documents the clinical rationale, epidemiological data, environmental burden, and protocol design logic behind BLEU's deployment in the city where it was born.

Published April 2026 Author Dr. Felicia Stoler, DCN RDN FACSM Publisher Jazz Bird 501(c)(3)
Contents
  1. Executive Summary
  2. Population and Demographics

01 — Executive Summary

Why New Orleans Needs a Different Kind of Health System

New Orleans, Louisiana is one of the most medically underserved major cities in the United States. It sits at the intersection of environmental catastrophe, generational trauma, chronic disease concentration, and systemic healthcare access failure. Louisiana ranks 49th nationally in overall health outcomes. Orleans Parish carries disproportionate burden even within this context.

BLEU was designed here because the need is here. The platform's AI clinical decision engine (ALVAI), 855,000 NPI-verified practitioner directory, drug interaction screening across 302,000 verified pairs, and evidence-based supplement protocols through Dr. Felicia Stoler's Fullscript dispensary were all architected to address the specific pathology of this city.

This whitepaper presents the epidemiological evidence, environmental data, and clinical reasoning that defines BLEU's protocol library for New Orleans. Every protocol on the platform traces back to the data documented here.
02 — Population and Demographics

The City by the Numbers

384K
Population
59%
Black / African American
33%
White
23.7%
Below Poverty Line
37.5
Median Age
$45K
Median Household Income

New Orleans is a majority-Black city with a poverty rate nearly double the national average (23.7% vs. 12.4%). The median household income of approximately $45,000 sits well below the national median of $75,000. These economic realities constrain healthcare access, nutrition quality, and preventive care engagement across every neighborhood from the Lower Ninth Ward to New Orleans East.

Post-Hurricane Katrina population displacement permanently altered the demographic composition. Roughly 100,000 residents who evacuated in 2005 never returned. The population that rebuilt carries compounded trauma, disrupted continuity of care, and fractured social support networks.

03 — Chronic Disease Burden

A City Under Metabolic Siege

Louisiana ranks among the worst states for obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and cancer mortality. New Orleans carries the weight of the state average while adding its own compounding factors: a food culture built on refined carbohydrates, saturated fat, and sodium; subtropical heat that limits outdoor physical activity for five months of the year; and a social culture deeply intertwined with alcohol consumption.

Chronic Disease Prevalence — Orleans Parish vs. National Average
Obesity (Adult)
36.2%
National Avg
31.9%
Diabetes (Type 2)
14.1%
National Avg
10.5%
Hypertension
38.4%
National Avg
29.4%
Heart Disease Mortality
221/100K
National Avg
168/100K

The metabolic syndrome cluster — obesity, insulin resistance, hypertension, and dyslipidemia — is not four separate problems in New Orleans. It is one interconnected cascade, driven by food environment, economic stress, and limited preventive care infrastructure. BLEU's protocol library addresses these as a system, not as isolated conditions.

04 — Environmental Health

Cancer Alley: 150 Petrochemical Plants Between Baton Rouge and New Orleans

The 85-mile corridor along the Mississippi River between Baton Rouge and New Orleans contains over 150 petrochemical plants and refineries. This region — known as Cancer Alley — exposes residents to elevated concentrations of benzene, formaldehyde, ethylene oxide, chloroprene, and other known carcinogens.

EPA data shows cancer risk in parts of St. James Parish and St. John the Baptist Parish at 50x the national average. New Orleans sits at the downstream terminus of this corridor. Airborne and waterborne toxicants do not stop at parish lines.
Environmental Exposure Data — Greater New Orleans
Petrochemical facilities within 50 miles150+
EPA Toxics Release Inventory sites89
Excess cancer risk (St. John Parish)50x national
Air Quality Index days above 100/yr18–32
Lead service lines remaining (est.)30,000+
Boil water advisories (2023–2025)12 events

Environmental toxicant exposure is not a background factor in New Orleans. It is a primary driver of disease. Chronic low-level exposure to volatile organic compounds drives oxidative stress, disrupts endocrine function, and increases baseline inflammation. BLEU's Immune Resilience Protocol and Longevity Core Protocol include antioxidant compounds (quercetin, resveratrol, omega-3 EPA/DHA) specifically because this city's environmental burden demands proactive cellular defense.

05 — Mental Health and Trauma Epidemiology

The Weight This City Carries

Hurricane Katrina in 2005 killed over 1,800 people and displaced 1.5 million across the Gulf Coast. Twenty-one years later, the trauma remains embedded in the nervous systems of survivors and their children. Post-Katrina PTSD prevalence was estimated at 30–50% among returning residents in the first five years. Longitudinal studies show elevated anxiety, depression, and substance use disorders persisting well beyond the acute aftermath.

28%
Mental Health Provider Access
22%
Adults Reporting Depression
19%
Binge Drinking Rate
24.1
Drug OD Deaths per 100K

The compounding trauma stack in New Orleans is not limited to Katrina. Hurricane Ida (2021) struck the same communities with Category 4 force. Chronic gun violence, particularly in underserved neighborhoods, creates ongoing acute stress exposure. The city's murder rate consistently ranks among the highest in the nation.

BLEU's mental health response operates on three levels. First, crisis detection: the Safety Shield system identifies suicidal ideation, self-harm language, and acute distress patterns and responds within 800ms with 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline routing. Second, the Anxiety Regulation Protocol and Mood Stabilization Protocol address the neurochemical consequences of chronic stress. Third, the practitioner directory surfaces NPI-verified psychiatrists, licensed clinical social workers, and addiction specialists within the New Orleans metro.

Only 28% of New Orleans adults who need mental health services can access them. BLEU does not replace therapists. BLEU fills the gap between needing help and finding it.
06 — Healthcare Access Gaps

The System That Is Not There

Louisiana expanded Medicaid in 2016, which insured approximately 500,000 previously uninsured residents. Despite this expansion, access barriers persist in New Orleans due to provider shortages, geographic maldistribution, and the concentration of specialists in affluent corridors while underserved neighborhoods are left with emergency rooms as primary care.

Access Barriers
Uninsured rate (Orleans)10.2%
Primary care physician shortageSevere
Mental health provider ratio350:1
Dentist shortage areas12 census tracts
Avg ER wait time4.2 hrs
BLEU Infrastructure Response
NPI practitioners indexed855,000+
Drug interaction pairs302,000
Protocol conditions covered10
GoodRx pharmacy pricingReal-time
Cost Plus Drug surfacingAlways first

Charity Hospital — the institution that served New Orleans' uninsured for 264 years — closed after Katrina and has never reopened as a full-service hospital. The healthcare safety net that existed before 2005 was not rebuilt to the same capacity. University Medical Center replaced it with a smaller facility. Community health centers have expanded but cannot absorb the full demand.

BLEU's finance module surfaces GoodRx real pharmacy pricing, Cost Plus Drug alternatives, patient assistance programs, HRSA health centers, and Medicaid eligibility checks because the cost of care is not an abstraction in this city. It is the reason people do not get care.

07 — Protocol Design Rationale

Why These Protocols for This City

BLEU's 10-protocol library was not designed generically and applied to New Orleans. It was designed for the pathology profile of this city and then extended to other locations with local adaptation. Every protocol traces to New Orleans epidemiology.

Protocol — Local Rationale Mapping
Sleep Reset ProtocolSubtropical heat disrupts circadian rhythm. 90°F+ nights May–Sept.
Cortisol + Stress ProtocolPost-Katrina chronic cortisol elevation. HPA axis dysregulation.
Gut Reset ProtocolNOLA food culture: high sodium, refined carbs, saturated fat.
Energy + Fatigue ProtocolHeat fatigue. B-vitamin depletion via alcohol culture.
Immune Resilience ProtocolCancer Alley oxidative stress. Chronic toxicant exposure.
Cognitive Focus ProtocolInflammation-driven cognitive fog. Sleep debt compounding.
Hormonal Support ProtocolEndocrine disruption from environmental toxicants.
Movement Recovery ProtocolHeat limits outdoor activity window to 6 months/year.
Longevity Core ProtocolAccelerated biological aging from combined stressor load.
Mood Stabilization ProtocolGenerational trauma. 22% depression prevalence.

All protocols are dispensed through Dr. Felicia Stoler's Fullscript account at 20–35% below retail. No SKUs are sold individually. Protocols are bundled as 30-day interventions because isolated supplementation without structure produces no measurable outcome. Every combination is screened through BLEU's drug interaction engine (CYP450, UGT, P-glycoprotein metabolic pathway data) before a user can purchase.

BLEU never recommends a product. BLEU recommends a protocol. The distinction is the difference between selling and treating.
08 — Clinical Authority and Governance

Who Stands Behind Every Output

Dr. Felicia Stoler
DCN · MS · RDN · FACSM · FAND · Dipl ACLM · Principal Investigator, Jazz Bird 501(c)(3)
Doctor of Clinical Nutrition. Registered Dietitian Nutritionist. Fellow of the American College of Sports Medicine. Fellow of the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics. Board-certified in Lifestyle Medicine. She audits every protocol on this platform for clinical accuracy, safety, and outcome integrity. She never prescribes. She certifies the knowledge infrastructure that serves you.

Jazz Bird 501(c)(3) is the nonprofit research entity that governs BLEU's clinical operations. Every user session in New Orleans that completes a 30-day protocol and checks in at Day 7 generates a causal data point for Jazz Bird's research. User outcomes — anonymized and aggregated — become the evidence base for future protocol refinement and grant applications.

BLEU's AI model routing ensures clinical-grade queries are handled by the appropriate model. Drug interactions, crisis detection, and complex research queries route to Claude Opus. Emotional synthesis and multi-step reasoning route to GPT-4o. Navigation and simple lookups route to GPT-4o Mini. No single model handles everything. The routing is the clinical judgment layer.

Clinical Governance Principles
BLEU never diagnosesAlways
Therapist disclaimer includedEvery session
Crisis detection override<800ms
Drug interaction screeningBefore purchase
iHerb sourcingPermanently excluded
Ad revenueZero. Ever.
09 — BLEU Technical Infrastructure

What Powers the System

BLEU operates as a single-page application backed by a Node.js HTTP server, a Deno-based edge function architecture (ALVAI 20-agent system with 6 Super-Fields), and a Python data pipeline pulling from 10 verified sources four times daily.

Data Sources (Pipeline)
NPI Registry855K+ practitioners
FDA / RxNormDrug interactions
Google PlacesLocal health services
PubMedClinical research
Open Food FactsNutrition data
AI Model Routing
GPT-4o Mini (70%)Navigation, lookups
GPT-4o (25%)Synthesis, emotion
Claude Opus (5%)Drug interactions, crisis
Intent detectionWord count + pattern
Safety Shield<800ms response

The ALVAI engine resolves — it does not respond. Every query produces: State (what the user is experiencing), Root Cause (1–2 biological drivers, ranked), Protocol (3–5 exact steps with timing), Execution (one CTA, one path), and Next Trigger (Day 7 check-in). This structure prevents the platform from devolving into a chatbot that validates without intervening.

The Longevity Stability Score (LSS) tracks 12 dimensions — nutrition, sleep architecture, stress regulation, movement, mental health, substance risk, preventive care, social connection, financial stress, environmental risk, medication adherence, and engagement — scored 0–100 across every session. The score is never shared with employers or insurers. It belongs to the user.

Start Your Protocol

Clinical-grade. Non-prescription. Dispensed through Dr. Stoler's Fullscript account at 20–35% below retail. Drug interaction screened before purchase.

Open BLEU Dispensary
10 — References

Sources and Citations

[1]America's Health Rankings, United Health Foundation. Louisiana state health rankings, 2025 edition.
[2]U.S. Census Bureau. American Community Survey 5-Year Estimates, Orleans Parish, Louisiana. 2020–2024.
[3]Louisiana Department of Health. Chronic Disease Prevalence Report, Orleans Parish. 2024.
[4]EPA Toxics Release Inventory (TRI) Program. Industrial District 85-mile Mississippi River Corridor data. 2023.
[5]Terrell KA, St. Julien G. "Air pollution is linked to higher cancer rates among black or impoverished communities in Louisiana." Environmental Research Letters, 2022; 17(1).
[6]Rhodes J et al. "The impact of Hurricane Katrina on the mental and physical health of low-income parents in New Orleans." American Journal of Orthopsychiatry, 2010; 80(2):237–247.
[7]Chandrasekhar D. "Diasporic disruption: post-Katrina population change and community resilience." Journal of Urban Affairs, 2012; 34(1):29–48.
[8]HRSA Health Professional Shortage Area (HPSA) Designations. Orleans Parish, Louisiana. Updated 2025.
[9]CDC PLACES Project. Local health outcome and risk factor estimates, Orleans Parish. 2024.
[10]National Practitioner Data Bank / NPI Registry. CMS National Plan and Provider Enumeration System. 2025.
[11]Pruss-Ustun A et al. "Preventing disease through healthy environments." WHO Report, 2016.
[12]Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA). National Survey on Drug Use and Health, Louisiana estimates. 2024.
